questions.question: Are strings mutable or immutable? What are the performance implications?
questions.answer: Strings are immutable in Python, JavaScript, and Java. Each modification creates a new string object. In a loop concatenating strings, this creates O(n) intermediate objects. The fix: use list.append() + "".join() in Python, or Array.join() in JavaScript, or StringBuilder in Java — all build the final string in one allocation.

questions.question: What is the difference between == and .equals() for strings in Java?
questions.answer: == compares object references (memory addresses). .equals() compares string content. Two string literals may point to the same String Pool object (== is True), but dynamically created strings (new String("x")) are separate heap objects. Always use .equals() for content comparison in Java.

questions.question: What is Unicode and why does it matter for strings?
questions.answer: Unicode is a standard that assigns a unique code point to every character in every human language. Python 3 strings are Unicode by default (sequence of code points). JavaScript uses UTF-16 encoding. This matters for emoji, multilingual apps, and internationalization — some emoji require 2 JavaScript "characters" (surrogate pairs) despite appearing as 1. String length in characters ≠ bytes ≠ code points in some encodings.

questions.question: What are the performance characteristics of high-frequency Web Development Troubleshooting Part 10C queries?
questions.answer: It depends on the data structure used. Average queries execute in O(1) time when using hash indexes, but can degrade to O(N) linear search if colliding.
